{{Short description|none}}
{{Lead too short|date=February 2025}}
'''Gender and disability''' examines the intersection of gender and disability, exploring how social, cultural, and economic factors shape the experiences of individuals with disabilities based on their gender identity. Women and non-binary individuals with disabilities often face compounded discrimination, experiencing both [[ableism]] and gender-based inequalities in areas such as healthcare, employment, education, and social participation. The intersectional nature of gender and disability influences access to resources, legal protections, and opportunities, with marginalized groups frequently encountering higher rates of poverty, violence, and exclusion.

Historically, gender norms and stereotypes have shaped perceptions of disability, often reinforcing traditional roles and limiting autonomy. Feminist and disability rights movements have increasingly addressed these issues, advocating for more inclusive policies and practices that recognize the diverse needs of disabled individuals across different gender identities. Research in this field spans disciplines such as sociology, gender studies, [[disability studies]], and public policy, aiming to challenge systemic barriers and promote equality.

== Medical misogyny ==

=== Historical ===

==== Hysteria ====
"[[Hysteria]] is undoubtedly the first mental disorder attributable to women, accurately described in the second millennium BC, and until Freud, considered an exclusively female disease."<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last1=Tasca |first1=Cecilia |last2=Rapetti |first2=Mariangela |last3=Carta |first3=Mauro Giovanni |last4=Fadda |first4=Bianca |date=2012-10-19 |title=Women And Hysteria In The History Of Mental Health |url=https://clinical-practice-and-epidemiology-in-mental-health.com/VOLUME/8/PAGE/110/ |journal=Clinical Practice & Epidemiology in Mental Health |language=en |volume=8 |issue=1 |pages=110–119 |doi=10.2174/1745017901208010110 |issn=1745-0179 |pmc=3480686 |pmid=23115576}}</ref> Since Ancient Greek times, the idea that a woman can go mad in a way that effects exclusively women was not an uncommon one, and until [[Sigmund Freud]] it was believed that often the reason for this madness was an improper sex life, with [[Plato]] arguing that the cure for hysteria was to "join with the male and... give rise to a new birth."<ref name=":0" /> [[Hippocrates]] wrote that the cause of hysteria was the buildup of putrefied [[Humorism|humors]] which caused various disorders within the body and could only be expunged through regular sexual behavior.{{cn|date=February 2025}}

In the Middle Ages, [[Trota of Salerno]] approached the idea of hysteria from a different perspective, offering the explanation that "the suffering related to gynecological diseases was "intimate": women often, out of shame, do not reveal their troubles to the doctor."<ref name=":0" /> Later, hysteria was a common reason for innocent women to be tried as witches and put to death.{{cn|date=February 2025}}

Freud recontextualizes the theory of hysteria, arguing instead that it is related to a dysfunction within the brain and not contained within the uterus, but that it is still a woman's disease, only serving to bring the concept of the "possessed woman" into the modern age.{{cn|date=February 2025}}

=== Modern ===
Many disabilities, such as [[Fibromyalgia]], are vastly disproportionally diagnosed in female patients compared to male patients even though there is no known medical cause for this gender disparity.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Yunus |first=Muhammad B. |date=2001-03-01 |title=The role of gender in fibromyalgia syndrome |url=https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11926-001-0008-3 |journal=Current Rheumatology Reports |language=en |volume=3 |issue=2 |pages=128–134 |doi=10.1007/s11926-001-0008-3 |pmid=11286669 |issn=1534-6307|url-access=subscription }}</ref> However, disorders involving the [[female reproductive system]] are often unnoticed, under-diagnosed, or reported to have diagnostic processes involving dissatisfactory amounts of delay or labor on behalf of the patient. These include [[Endometriosis]]<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Davenport |first1=Sophie |last2=Smith |first2=Diane |last3=Green |first3=Dan J. |date=2023-07-13 |title=Barriers to a Timely Diagnosis of Endometriosis: A Qualitative Systematic Review |url=https://journals.lww.com/10.1097/AOG.0000000000005255 |journal=Obstetrics & Gynecology |volume=142 |issue=3 |pages=571–583 |language=en |doi=10.1097/AOG.0000000000005255 |pmid=37441792 |issn=0029-7844|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Ballard |first1=Karen |last2=Lowton |first2=Karen |last3=Wright |first3=Jeremy |date=November 2006 |title=What's the delay? A qualitative study of women's experiences of reaching a diagnosis of endometriosis |journal=Fertility and Sterility |volume=86 |issue=5 |pages=1296–1301 |doi=10.1016/j.fertnstert.2006.04.054 |pmid=17070183 |issn=0015-0282|doi-access=free }}</ref> and [[Polycystic ovary syndrome]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Gibson-Helm |first1=Melanie |last2=Teede |first2=Helena |last3=Dunaif |first3=Andrea |last4=Dokras |first4=Anuja |date=December 2016 |title=Delayed diagnosis and a lack of information associated with dissatisfaction in women with polycystic ovary syndrome |journal=The Journal of Clinical Endocrinology & Metabolism |volume=102 |issue=2 |pages=jc.2016–2963 |doi=10.1210/jc.2016-2963 |issn=0021-972X |pmc=6283441 |pmid=27906550}}</ref>

Disabilities common to both females and males, especially developmental disabilities, often favor male patients in ease of diagnosis. Studies done on both [[autism]]<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Cruz |first1=Sara |last2=Zubizarreta |first2=Sabela Conde-Pumpido |last3=Costa |first3=Ana Daniela |last4=Araújo |first4=Rita |last5=Martinho |first5=Júlia |last6=Tubío-Fungueiriño |first6=María |last7=Sampaio |first7=Adriana |last8=Cruz |first8=Raquel |last9=Carracedo |first9=Angel |last10=Fernández-Prieto |first10=Montse |date=2024-01-29 |title=Is There a Bias Towards Males in the Diagnosis of Autism? A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis |journal=Neuropsychology Review |volume=35 |issue=1 |pages=153–176 |language=en |doi=10.1007/s11065-023-09630-2 |pmid=38285291 |issn=1573-6660|doi-access=free |pmc=11965184 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Hull |first1=Laura |last2=Petrides |first2=K. V. |last3=Mandy |first3=William |date=2020-12-01 |title=The Female Autism Phenotype and Camouflaging: a Narrative Review |journal=Review Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders |language=en |volume=7 |issue=4 |pages=306–317 |doi=10.1007/s40489-020-00197-9 |issn=2195-7185|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Lockwood Estrin |first1=Georgia |last2=Milner |first2=Victoria |last3=Spain |first3=Debbie |last4=Happé |first4=Francesca |last5=Colvert |first5=Emma |date=2021-12-01 |title=Barriers to Autism Spectrum Disorder Diagnosis for Young Women and Girls: a Systematic Review |journal=Review Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders |language=en |volume=8 |issue=4 |pages=454–470 |doi=10.1007/s40489-020-00225-8 |issn=2195-7185 |pmc=8604819 |pmid=34868805}}</ref> and [[attention deficit hyperactivity disorder]]<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Mowlem |first1=Florence |last2=Agnew-Blais |first2=Jessica |last3=Taylor |first3=Eric |last4=Asherson |first4=Philip |date=2019-02-01 |title=Do different factors influence whether girls versus boys meet ADHD diagnostic criteria? Sex differences among children with high ADHD symptoms |journal=Psychiatry Research |volume=272 |pages=765–773 |doi=10.1016/j.psychres.2018.12.128 |issn=0165-1781 |pmc=6401208 |pmid=30832197}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last1=Bruchmüller |first1=Katrin |last2=Margraf |first2=Jürgen |last3=Schneider |first3=Silvia |date=2012 |title=Is ADHD diagnosed in accord with diagnostic criteria? Overdiagnosis and influence of client gender on diagnosis. |url=https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/a0026582 |journal=Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology |language=en |volume=80 |issue=1 |pages=128–138 |doi=10.1037/a0026582 |pmid=22201328 |issn=1939-2117|url-access=subscription }}</ref> suggest that "masculine defaults" <ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Cheryan |first1=Sapna |last2=Markus |first2=Hazel Rose |date=November 2020 |title=Masculine defaults: Identifying and mitigating hidden cultural biases. |journal=Psychological Review |language=en |volume=127 |issue=6 |pages=1022–1052 |doi=10.1037/rev0000209 |pmid=32804526 |issn=1939-1471|doi-access=free }}</ref> in healthcare influence diagnosticians by creating a mental [[Theory of forms|platonic ideal]] diagnostic case, which often excludes women who present the same symptoms as men.<ref name=":1" />

==== Interactions with racism ====
Stemming from [[Medical racism]], there is a common phenomenon experienced by [[people of color]] who seek medical attention where their bodies are believed to be fundamentally different than those of white people by white people who work in medical fields.<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last=Hogarth |first=Rana Asali |date=October 2019 |title=The Myth of Innate Racial Differences Between White and Black People's Bodies: Lessons From the 1793 Yellow Fever Epidemic in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania |journal=American Journal of Public Health |language=en |volume=109 |issue=10 |pages=1339–1341 |doi=10.2105/AJPH.2019.305245 |issn=0090-0036 |pmc=6727282 |pmid=31415198}}</ref> One such particularly pervasive belief is the idea that "blacks have thicker skin than do white people,<ref name=":2" />" which leads to the idea that Black patients have a higher innate pain tolerance than white patients.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Hoffman |first1=Kelly M. |last2=Trawalter |first2=Sophie |last3=Axt |first3=Jordan R. |last4=Oliver |first4=M. Norman |date=2016-04-19 |title=Racial bias in pain assessment and treatment recommendations, and false beliefs about biological differences between blacks and whites |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |language=en |volume=113 |issue=16 |pages=4296–4301 |doi=10.1073/pnas.1516047113 |doi-access=free |issn=0027-8424 |pmc=4843483 |pmid=27044069|bibcode=2016PNAS..113.4296H }}</ref>

Similarly to the seen-above idea of the male default, there exists a parallel white-as-default <ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Petsko |first1=Christopher D. |last2=Rosette |first2=Ashleigh Shelby |date=February 2023 |title=Are leaders still presumed white by default? Racial bias in leader categorization revisited. |url=https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/apl0001020 |journal=Journal of Applied Psychology |language=en |volume=108 |issue=2 |pages=330–340 |doi=10.1037/apl0001020 |pmid=35467925 |issn=1939-1854|url-access=subscription }}</ref> which influences medical practice. Medical devices such as the [[Pulse oximetry|Pulse oximeter]] are calibrated to white skin, causing them to read improperly when used on Black patients.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Moran-Thomas |first=Amy |date=August 5, 2020 |title=How a Popular Medical Device Encodes Racial Bias |url=https://www.bostonreview.net/articles/amy-moran-thomas-pulse-oximeter/ |work=Boston Review}}</ref>  Diagnostic tests, such as kidney function screens, read Black patients as having higher risk when tested.<ref name=":3">{{Cite journal |last1=Sederstrom |first1=Nneka |last2=Lasege |first2=Tamika |date=March 2022 |title=Anti-Black Racism as a Chronic Condition |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/hast.1364 |journal=Hastings Center Report |language=en |volume=52 |issue=S1 |pages=S24–S29 |doi=10.1002/hast.1364 |pmid=35470878 |issn=0093-0334|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Often, medical training itself is oriented around light skin as a standard.<ref name=":3" /> These and additional factors, when amplified across an entire anti-Black medical system, end up treating Blackness itself as a [[chronic condition]].<ref name=":3" />

Black patients have described the way their concerns about themselves and their family members are dismissed by the medical profession as [[Gaslighting]].<ref name=":4">{{Cite journal |last=Carter |first=Chelsey R. |date=November 2022 |title=Gaslighting: ALS, anti-Blackness, and medicine |url=https://anthrosource.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/fea2.12107 |journal=Feminist Anthropology |language=en |volume=3 |issue=2 |pages=235–245 |doi=10.1002/fea2.12107 |issn=2643-7961|url-access=subscription }}</ref> For example, [[ALS]], on average, is diagnosed later in Black patients than equivalent white ones,<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Brand |first1=David |last2=Polak |first2=Meraida |last3=Glass |first3=Jonathan D. |last4=Fournier |first4=Christina N. |date=2021-02-09 |title=Comparison of Phenotypic Characteristics and Prognosis Between Black and White Patients in a Tertiary ALS Clinic |url=https://www.neurology.org/doi/10.1212/WNL.0000000000011396 |journal=Neurology |language=en |volume=96 |issue=6 |pages=e840–e844 |doi=10.1212/WNL.0000000000011396 |pmid=33372030 |issn=0028-3878|url-access=subscription }}</ref> if not missed entirely.<ref name=":4" />  Studies have found systematic barriers to breast cancer diagnosis in Black women.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Jones |first1=Claire EL |last2=Maben |first2=Jill |last3=Jack |first3=Ruth H. |last4=Davies |first4=Elizabeth A. |last5=Forbes |first5=Lindsay JL |last6=Lucas |first6=Grace |last7=Ream |first7=Emma |date=2014-02-01 |title=A systematic review of barriers to early presentation and diagnosis with breast cancer among black women |url=https://bmjopen.bmj.com/content/4/2/e004076 |journal=BMJ Open |language=en |volume=4 |issue=2 |article-number=e004076 |doi=10.1136/bmjopen-2013-004076  |doi-access=free|issn=2044-6055 |pmc=3927711 |pmid=24523424}}</ref> Ethnic minority patients diagnosed with [[fibromyalgia]] report higher levels of sleep and mood disturbances than white patients.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Marr |first1=Nicole C. |last2=Liew |first2=Charles Van |last3=Carovich |first3=Tessa F. |last4=Cecchini |first4=Gianna A. |last5=McKinley |first5=Lauren E. |last6=Cronan |first6=Terry A. |date=2020-04-16 |title=The Effects of Racial/Ethnic Minority Status on Sleep, Mood Disturbance, and Depression in People with Fibromyalgia |journal=Psychology Research and Behavior Management |language=English |volume=13 |pages=343–353 |doi=10.2147/PRBM.S242699 |doi-access=free |pmc=7174195 |pmid=32368163}}</ref>

In following with the concept of [[misogynoir]], Black female patients, explained by [[Intersectionality|intersectional]] feminism, suffer both from racism and sexism within healthcare and often find that the combination of the two synthesizes into something separate from each on their own. College-educated Black mothers are nearly six times as likely to die from pregnancy-related complications, and as the rate of fatal complications decrease in white patients correlating with education level, it stays nearly the same among Black patients.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-12-16 |title=Maternal Mortality in the United States: A Primer |url=https://www.commonwealthfund.org/publications/issue-brief-report/2020/dec/maternal-mortality-united-states-primer |access-date=2024-10-17 |website=www.commonwealthfund.org |language=en |doi=10.26099/ta1q-mw24 |last1=Declercq |first1=Eugene |last2=Zephyrin |first2=Laurie }}</ref>

Significant parts of the profession of [[Gynaecology]] are reliant on information that was obtained by using the bodies of Black women as testing equipment, only to dispose of them after the information had been gleaned, often without medical treatment.<ref name=":5">{{Cite book |last=Owens |first=Deirdre Cooper |title=Medical Bondage: Race, Gender, and the Origins of American Gynecology |date=2017-11-15 |publisher=University of Georgia Press |isbn=978-0-8203-5303-6 |doi=10.2307/j.ctt1pwt69x|jstor=j.ctt1pwt69x }}</ref> [[J. Marion Sims]], often referred to with the epithet "Father of Gynecology", was notorious for operating on enslaved women without anesthesia, a practice common among medical professionals at the time.<ref name=":5" />

== In transgender people ==
[[Transgender]] patients seeking medical care, especially those on [[Hormone replacement therapy]], are often subject to what is known as [[Trans broken arm syndrome]], a phenomenon in which "a provider incorrectly assumes that a medical condition results from a patient's gender identity or medical transition."<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Wall |first1=Catherine S. J. |last2=Patev |first2=Alison J. |last3=Benotsch |first3=Eric G. |date=2023-03-01 |title=Trans broken arm syndrome: A mixed-methods exploration of gender-related medical misattribution and invasive questioning |journal=Social Science & Medicine |volume=320 |article-number=115748 |doi=10.1016/j.socscimed.2023.115748 |pmid=36736052 |issn=0277-9536|doi-access=free }}</ref>

Transgender people are nearly twice as likely to be disabled when compared to [[cisgender]] people.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Smith-Johnson |first=Madeline |date=2022-10-01 |title=Transgender Adults Have Higher Rates Of Disability Than Their Cisgender Counterparts: Study examines rates of disability among transgender adults and cisgender adults. |journal=Health Affairs |language=en |volume=41 |issue=10 |pages=1470–1476 |doi=10.1377/hlthaff.2022.00500 |pmid=36190882 |issn=0278-2715|doi-access=free }}</ref> In studies of trans youth, it has been reported that trans people are more likely to have [[Ehlers–Danlos syndrome]]<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Jones |first1=Jordan T. |last2=Black |first2=William R. |last3=Moser |first3=Christine N. |last4=Rush |first4=Eric T. |last5=Malloy Walton |first5=Lindsey |date=January 2022 |title=Gender dysphoria in adolescents with Ehlers–Danlos syndrome |journal=SAGE Open Medicine |language=en |volume=10 |article-number=20503121221146074 |doi=10.1177/20503121221146074 |issn=2050-3121 |pmc=9806399 |pmid=36600979}}</ref> and [[Psychosis]],<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Barr |first1=Sebastian M. |last2=Roberts |first2=Dominic |last3=Thakkar |first3=Katharine N. |date=2021-12-01 |title=Psychosis in transgender and gender non-conforming individuals: A review of the literature and a call for more research |url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S0165178121005679 |journal=Psychiatry Research |volume=306 |article-number=114272 |doi=10.1016/j.psychres.2021.114272 |pmid=34808496 |issn=0165-1781|url-access=subscription }}</ref> among other disabilities.

== Citations ==
<references />

[[Category:Disability and gender| ]]
